steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage states that vocabulary accompanying crops as they spread is a global phenomenon, then pivots with “yet” to introduce a contradiction. The blank must supply evidence showing that corn vocabulary did NOT follow the crop into the Southeast — that is, data proving decoupling.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘the origins of vocabulary pertaining to the crop vary… showing no demonstrable relationship to Totozoquean vocabulary’: directly proves the crop spread without its Totozoquean vocabulary, completing the “yet” contrast. ✓
  • Choice B — ‘the region is linguistically diverse, being home not only to Chitimacha and Caddo…’: catalogues regional languages but says nothing about the origin of corn-related vocabulary; does not complete the contrast. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘corn-related vocabulary underwent changes when entering other, unrelated languages…’: shows vocabulary that did travel and transform, which supports the phenomenon the passage is contrasting against, not the exception. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘words for corn in the languages of the Muskogean family evolved from a common root…’: describes internal relationships within Muskogean and says nothing about whether those words relate to Totozoquean vocabulary. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
